title: The Vineyard and the Blood-Stained Tenants
subtitle: The householder plants his vineyard, sends his servants, and at last his Son — who is cast outside the wall and killed

stillness: Be still before the Lord of the vineyard.
Let the noise of the day fall away.
A vineyard has been entrusted to you — listen.
gospel: Hear another parable.
There was a householder who planted a vineyard,
and set a hedge around it,
and dug in it a winepress,
and built a tower,
and let it out to husbandmen;
and went into a far country.
When the time of the fruits drew near,
he sent his servants to receive its fruits.
And the husbandmen, seizing his servants,
beat one, killed another, and stoned another.
Again he sent other servants, more than the first,
and they did to them in like manner.
But last of all he sent to them his Son,
saying: They will reverence my Son.
But the husbandmen, seeing the Son, said within themselves:
This is the heir; come, let us kill him,
and we shall have his inheritance.
And taking him, they cast him out of the vineyard,
and killed him.
Therefore I say to you:
the kingdom of God shall be taken from you,
and given to a nation yielding its fruits.
behold: Behold the householder who is God: by nature the Lord, by good will a Father.
He planted his vineyard — the house of Israel, the synagogue —
from the chosen vines of the Patriarchs and Prophets,
carried over out of Egypt and set in the land of promise.
He set a threefold hedge around it:
the guardianship of Angels over the walls of Jerusalem,
the protection of men of war,
and the rampart of the city walls.
He dug in it a winepress — the law of justice,
the fear that presses down and torments the wicked.
He built a tower — the Temple for the divine worship,
called a tower for the safety of its refuge.
And he let it out to husbandmen — to the priests and princes,
who ought to tear out vices like thorns,
and to graft in virtues like good seed.
He went into a far country: not by changing place, for God is everywhere,
but he left the vinedressers their free will,
and bore long with them, waiting for them to turn from their sins.
When the time of fruit drew near, he sent his servants — the Prophets;
and the husbandmen seized them: one they beat, one they killed, one they stoned.
Again he sent more Prophets, and they did to them the same.
Last of all he sent his Son, incarnate for us,
whom, knowing him to be the heir and the prince of all things,
they cast outside the wall — outside the gate of Jerusalem —
and crucified him, that the inheritance might be their own.
For they thought within themselves: if this people take him for their Lord,
we lose the custom of the victims that is prepared for our gain.
But if we kill him, the custom of offering sacrifices will be held forever,
and so it will become our possession, and our inheritance.
consider: See the malice and ingratitude of the wicked tenants, and search your own heart.
On these ungrateful ones God had heaped so many benefits — and they returned blood.
They killed the servants; they killed the Son; they would seize what was never theirs.
And the Lord came to judge, and to avenge this deed:
the army of the Romans came against them by his ordering,
and the wicked he destroyed wickedly,
when their city and their Temple were thrown down,
and the people slain and led away captive.
But know this, wretched sinner: that vineyard is also your own soul,
let out to you by God, poured into the body to make the fruit of good works.
And whenever Christ the Son is sent invisibly by the Father,
stirring you toward the good — there are ungrateful ones still,
who not only repel his prompting but do most wickedly;
and so, as much as in them lies, they crucify him again.
Of these the Apostle says: they crucify again the Son of God.
Each one of us is the vineyard, and each one is also the vinedresser,
for every one of us tills his own self.
Happy are they who till well and do not neglect,
who increase and do not diminish,
who keep and do not destroy.
prayer: A vineyard has been let out to us, O Lord,
and let out on this condition:
that we render the fruit to you in its seasons.
Let us know, each one of us, that we must give back what is yours —
not the glory to ourselves, but to you the glory of God.
Make us happy tenants who till well and do not neglect,
who increase and do not diminish,
who keep and do not destroy;
that we be not cast into the outer darkness,
but render the fruit to you in its seasons.

latinJewel.latin: Felices qui bene colunt et non negligunt, augent et non diminuunt, conservant et non destruunt!
latinJewel.english: Happy are they who till well and do not neglect, who increase and do not diminish, who keep and do not destroy!
